era_context: The early 14th century continues with the Yuan Dynasty in China under Yesün Temür; the Mamluk Sultanate dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; Western Europe is fragmented among feudal monarchies with the Avignon Papacy as a major political actor; the Mali Empire under Mansa Musa is at its height; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Teutonic Order controls the Baltic region.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; reliance on later Arab geographies)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; Chinese sources more reliable)
Oceania and Pacific (virtually no written records)
Eastern Europe and the Steppe (limited Western documentation; Russian chronicles underrepresented)

events.title: Edward III becomes King of England; deposition of Edward II
events.description: Edward III, aged 14, becomes King of England following the deposition and death of his father Edward II. Edward II's reign had been marked by military failures (loss of Scotland), factional conflicts, and the dominance of unpopular favorites (Piers Gaveston, Hugh Despenser). Edward III's accession, orchestrated by his mother Isabella and her ally Roger Mortimer, marks a shift toward more effective royal governance. Edward III's long reign (1327–1377) will be defined by military ambitions in Scotland and France, and the development of English parliamentary institutions.

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Edward III's accession in 1327 is well-documented in contemporary English records and later chronicles.

events.title: Battle of Bannockburn aftermath; Scottish independence consolidation
events.description: Following the Scottish victory at Bannockburn (1314), Scotland under Robert I (Robert the Bruce) consolidates its independence from English rule. By 1327, the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328, but negotiations ongoing in 1327) formally recognizes Scottish independence. Edward III's accession in 1327 marks a shift in English policy; the young king's focus turns toward France rather than Scotland. Scottish independence, secured through military victory and diplomatic negotiation, becomes established fact.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland, r. 1306–1329)
Edward III (newly crowned King of England)
Thomas Randolph, Earl of Moray (Scottish diplomat)
events.certainty_note: Scottish independence is confirmed by the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328). The consolidation process in 1327 is inferred from diplomatic records and later chronicles.

events.title: Ottoman capture of Bursa (or consolidation of control)
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Osman I or his successor Orhan I, captures or consolidates control of Bursa, a major Byzantine city in northwestern Anatolia. The dating of this event is disputed: some sources place it in 1326, others in 1327 or later. Bursa becomes the first major Ottoman capital and a symbol of Ottoman expansion at Byzantine expense. The capture marks a significant step in the Ottoman rise to power and the decline of Byzantine territorial control in Anatolia.

sources.name: Byzantine sources (Pachymeres, Gregoras), contemporary or near-contemporary

events.certainty_note: The capture of Bursa is traditionally dated to 1326, but some sources place it in 1327 or later. Ottoman chronicles are legendary and were compiled centuries later. Byzantine sources provide some contemporary corroboration but are limited in detail.

events.title: Mansa Musa's return to Mali; consolidation of Islamic governance
events.description: Mansa Musa, having completed his hajj to Mecca in 1324, continues to consolidate Islamic institutions and governance in Mali. By 1327, Islamic scholars and architects brought from the Hajj are establishing mosques, schools, and administrative systems based on Islamic law. Timbuktu and Gao are emerging as major centers of Islamic learning and trade. This period marks Mali's transformation into a major Islamic power in West Africa and a center of Islamic scholarship.

sources.name: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (travel account, written c. 1355 but describing 1324–1354 travels)
Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar (written c. 1340, describing Mali c. 1324–1330)

events.certainty_note: Mali's Islamic institutional development is inferred from later Arab geographies and Ibn Battuta's account (written decades after his visit). Direct contemporary documentation is sparse.

events.title: Yesün Temür's reign in Yuan Dynasty; internal factional conflicts
events.description: Yesün Temür (also known as Taiding Khan), Khan of the Great Mongols and Emperor of the Yuan Dynasty, continues his reign amid internal factional conflicts. His rule is marked by struggles between Mongol princes, Chinese bureaucratic interests, and military factions. By 1327, the Yuan Dynasty's internal stability is increasingly fragile, with regional warlords gaining autonomy and the central government's authority weakening. Yesün Temür's death in 1328 will precipitate a succession crisis.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty), compiled 14th–15th century
Chinese historiographic consensus on Yuan Dynasty

events.certainty_note: Yesün Temür's reign and the internal conflicts of the Yuan Dynasty are well-attested in official histories, though these were compiled after the dynasty's fall.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Ottoman capture of Bursa is disputed. While 1326 is commonly cited, some sources place it in 1327 or later (as late as 1331). Ottoman chronicles are legendary and unreliable for precise dating. Additionally, the 'capture' of Bursa is sometimes attributed to Osman I, but modern scholarship increasingly credits Orhan I with this conquest. The significance of Edward III's accession is sometimes overstated as a 'restoration' of English power; Edward III's early reign was marked by continued conflicts with Scotland and France, and his military ambitions were not immediately successful. The Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328) is sometimes presented as a definitive settlement of Scottish independence, but English claims to Scottish overlordship persisted and led to renewed conflicts in the 14th century.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1327 is uneven across regions. European sources (English royal records, papal documents, Scottish chronicles) are relatively rich, though Scottish sources are often compiled centuries later. Chinese sources (Yuan official histories) are substantial but were compiled by the succeeding Ming Dynasty and reflect Ming biases. Ottoman sources are legendary and were compiled centuries later; Byzantine sources provide some contemporary corroboration but are limited in scope. West African sources are sparse and mediated through later Arab geographies. The historical record is heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Court-sponsored narratives dominate; the perspectives of non-elite populations are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: Edward III's accession follows the deposition of Edward II in 1327
Edward III's accession begins his long reign, which ends in 1377
Scottish consolidation of independence follows the victory at Bannockburn in 1314
Ottoman expansion continues under Osman I and Orhan I
Mali's Islamic institutional development follows Mansa Musa's hajj in 1324
Yesün Temür's reign continues from his accession in 1323
